ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for daging masak merah; mapped to the broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for daging masak merah; A0CKP (beef, cooked, with sauce) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Malay beef dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of daging masak merah is approximately 150 g, equivalent to about 3–4 pieces of braised beef with sauce, as commonly plated alongside steamed rice in a Singaporean Malay meal.

nutrient_source.title: Daging masak merah — HPB Singapore Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for daging masak merah (cr_id F06040015). Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef stew with tomato-based sauce (USDA SR Legacy #17158) as the closest approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for daging masak merah. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed beef stew with tomato-based sauce and moderate sugar content. The carbohydrate contribution is primarily from tomatoes, chilli paste, and added sugar; the presence of protein (19.6 g/100g), fat (8.4 g/100g), and fibre (1.4 g/100g) attenuates glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (45 × 11.21 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.6 g. Reference: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for beef stew with vegetables.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 452 mg/100g combined with high sugar (11.0 g/100g) and low dietary fibre (1.4 g/100g), triggering insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced salt and sugar in preparation, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset sodium load; the combination of 452 mg sodium and 11 g sugar per 100 g makes this dish a high-concern food for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 11.0 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.4 g/100g) in a red-meat dish; moderate glycaemic load of 7.6 per 150 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume daging masak merah in modest portions (100–120 g) alongside high-fibre vegetables and minimise added sugar in preparation, as the 11 g/100g sugar content with only 1.4 g/100g fibre can elevate post-meal blood glucose despite the attenuating effect of protein and fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) with estimated saturated fat ~3.8 g/100g, sodium 452 mg/100g, and coconut milk contributing additional saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, daging masak merah should be consumed occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), with preparation modifications to reduce coconut milk and salt, as the combination of red meat, saturated fat from coconut milk, and high sodium presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (arachidonic acid, pro-inflammatory omega-6), coconut milk (saturated fat), and nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, chilli) may trigger RA flares
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the pro-inflammatory combination of red meat rich in arachidonic acid, saturated fat from coconut milk, and nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, chilli) that may exacerbate joint inflammation, though turmeric and ginger in the spice base provide partial anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Daging masak merah is a traditional Singaporean Malay and Malaysian braised beef dish in which tender cuts of beef are slow-simmered in a richly spiced red sauce made from blended chilli paste, fresh tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, lemongrass, and coconut milk, seasoned with turmeric, sugar, and salt. The name translates literally from Malay as 'red-cooked meat'. The dish is characterised by a deep brick-red colour from the chilli and tomato base, a mildly spicy and slightly sweet flavour profile, and a tender, fall-apart beef texture achieved through prolonged braising. It is a staple at Malay weddings, festive occasions, and everyday family meals, typically served alongside steamed white rice or nasi lemak. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 199 kcal, 19.6 g protein, 8.4 g fat, 11.2 g carbohydrate, 11.0 g sugar, 1.4 g dietary fibre, and 452 mg sodium. The high protein content makes it a satisfying main dish, but the elevated sodium (452 mg/100g) and sugar (11 g/100g) with low fibre (1.4 g/100g) make it a concern for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in its traditional preparation and is gluten-free. Iron content is notable at 4.41 mg/100g, contributing to daily iron requirements. Clinically, the sugar-to-fibre ratio warrants caution for diabetics, and sodium content requires portion control for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: Is daging masak merah suitable for diabetics?
Can hypertensive patients eat daging masak merah?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06040015, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from analogous food in the University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional Malay beef preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F06040015, and no equivalent entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy #17158 (beef stew with tomato-based sauce) as the closest approved secondary source and is flagged as an estimate. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for daging masak merah was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. Chinese local name (红烧牛肉 / 紅燒牛肉) is a descriptive translation ('red-braised beef') used as the closest Chinese-language equivalent for this Malay dish; no established Chinese loanword exists for daging masak merah in the Singapore Chinese community context.
